title: STANDARD FOR CANNED SHRIMPS OR PRAWNS CXS 37-1991 Adopted in 1991. Revised in 1995. Amended in 2011, 2013, 2016, 2018.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR CANNED SHRIMPS OR PRAWNS CXS 37-1991 Adopted in 1991. Revised in 1995. Amended in 2011, 2013, 2016, 2018.
sections.summary: Document title: Codex Standard for Canned Shrimps or Prawns (CXS 37-1991) with adoption and amendment history (1991; revised 1995; amended 2011, 2013, 2016, 2018).
Scope: applies to canned shrimps/prawns (hereafter “shrimp”) and excludes specialty products where shrimp constitutes less than 50% m/m of the contents.

Description: defines the canned shrimp product (species families listed), requires hermetically sealed containers and commercial sterility, and specifies presentations (peeled, cleaned/de‑veined, broken) and size designation options (labelled count range or Annex B); other presentations are permitted if distinctive and adequately labelled.
Product definition: canned shrimp are prepared from one or more species of the Penaeidae, Pandalidae, Crangonidae and Palaemonidae families with heads, shell and antennae removed.
Process definition: canned shrimp must be packed in hermetically sealed containers and receive a processing treatment sufficient to ensure commercial sterility.

Presentation: product presentations include peeled (headed and peeled, dorsal tract retained), cleaned/de‑veined (back cut open and dorsal tract removed at least to last segment, comprising 95% of shrimp contents), and broken (more than 10% of contents are pieces under four segments); other presentations allowed if distinctive, compliant with the Standard and clearly labelled, and size may be declared by count or follow Annex B.

Other forms of presentation: any non‑standard presentation is permitted only if it is clearly distinctive from prescribed presentations, meets all Standard requirements, and is adequately described on the label to avoid misleading consumers.
Size: canned shrimp may be designated by actual count range declared on the label or by size provisions given in Annex B.

Essential composition and quality factors: shrimp must be from the listed species and of quality fit for sale as fresh for human consumption; packing medium and other ingredients must be food‑grade and meet applicable Codex standards; final product conformity is assessed by lot examination per Section 9 against provisions in Section 8 using methods in Section 7.
Shrimp quality: raw shrimp must be sound and of a quality fit to be sold fresh for human consumption and from the species listed in Section 2.1.
Other ingredients: the packing medium and all other ingredients must be food‑grade and conform to all applicable Codex standards.
Final product conformity: products are compliant with this Standard when lots examined per Section 9 meet the provisions of Section 8 and are tested by the methods specified in Section 7.

Food additives: permitted use of acidity regulators, colours and sequestrants in accordance with Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192‑1995) for food category 09.4, and only specified Table 3 acidity regulators as indicated in that Standard.

Hygiene: preparation and handling should follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice (including fishery and canned low‑acid codes), and products should comply with microbiological criteria established under CXG 21‑1997.

Labelling (heading): in addition to the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods, specific labelling rules for canned shrimp cover the product name, presentation qualifiers, size declarations and broken‑shrimp labelling.

Name of the food: the label must name the product "shrimp" or "prawns" (optionally with species common/usual name consistent with law/custom and not misleading), qualify the name by presentation per Sections 2.3.1–2.3.4, comply with size labelling rules (Section 2.3.5 and Annex B) and label products meeting the definition of "broken shrimp" as such.

Sampling, examination and analyses: (1) lot sampling for final product inspection must follow an appropriate plan with AQL‑6.5 and net/drained weight sampling must meet CAC criteria; (2) sensory/physical assessments must be performed by trained personnel (Annex A and CXG 31‑1999), and the Standard prescribes specific procedures for determination of net weight, drained weight and size (number per 100 g drained).

Sampling: lot sampling for final product examination shall follow an appropriate sampling plan with an AQL of 6.5, and sampling for net weight and drained weight shall follow an appropriate plan meeting criteria established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.

Sensoric and physical examination: samples for sensory and physical assessment must be evaluated by trained persons in accordance with Annex A and the Guidelines for the Sensory Evaluation of Fish and Shellfish in Laboratories (CXG 31‑1999).

Determination of net weight: net content is obtained by weighing the unopened container, removing and discarding contents, weighing the empty container (after removing excess liquid and adhering meat) and subtracting the empty container weight from the unopened container weight.

Specifies the procedure to determine drained weight of canned shrimp sample units: condition container at 20–30°C for ≥12 h, pour contents onto a pre-weighed circular sieve (2.8×2.8 mm mesh) inclined ~17–20°, allow to drain for 2 minutes, weigh sieve plus drained shrimp and subtract sieve weight to obtain drained weight.
Defines size designation as the number of shrimp per 100 g of drained product calculated by (number of whole shrimp in the unit ÷ actual drained weight of the unit) × 100.

States that a sample unit is defective if it fails the final product requirements in Section 3.3 and introduces Section 8’s defect categories—foreign matter, odour/flavour, texture, discolouration and objectionable matter—with criteria for each.

Defines foreign matter as any material not derived from shrimp that does not pose a human-health threat but is readily recognizable without magnification or is present at a level (detectable by any method, including magnification) indicating non‑compliance with good manufacturing or sanitation practices.
Defines odour/flavour defects as persistent and distinct objectionable odours or flavours indicative of decomposition or rancidity in the sample unit.
Defines texture defects as either excessively mushy flesh or excessively tough flesh that are uncharacteristic of the species/presentation.
Defines discolouration as distinct blackening on more than 10% of an individual shrimp’s surface area that affects over 15% of the shrimp in the sample unit.
Classifies objectionable matter to include struvite crystals and deems a sample unit defective if any struvite crystal exceeds 5 mm in length.

Sets lot acceptance criteria: the total number of defectives (per Section 8) and the number of units not meeting presentation (Section 2.3) must not exceed the acceptance number c from an appropriate sampling plan at AQL 6.5; the average net and drained weights must be at least the declared weights with no unreasonable shortage per container; and Sections 4, 5 and 6 (Food Additives, Hygiene and Labelling) must be met.

Specifies sensory and physical examination steps: inspect containers for integrity, determine weight per Sections 7.3/7.4, remove product and determine size per Section 7.5, inspect for discolouration/foreign/objectionable matter, and assess odour, flavour and texture following CXG 31-1999.
Annex B provides size-designation terms for canned shrimp (extra large/jumbo, large, medium, small, tiny) with prescribed ranges expressed as number of whole shrimp per 100 g drained product.
Defines canned shrimp size designations by whole shrimp per 100 g drained product: Extra Large/Jumbo ≤13; Large 14–19; Medium 20–34; Small 35–65; Tiny >65.
Non‑substantive heading for Annex A (no substantive requirements provided).
